title: Speeding up agentic workflows with WebSockets in the Responses API
optimized_headline: Enhancing Agentic Workflows: Discover WebSockets in the Responses API
raw_excerpt: A deep dive into the Codex agent loop, showing how WebSockets and connection-scoped caching reduced API overhead and improved model latency.
raw_body: A deep dive into the Codex agent loop, showing how WebSockets and connection-scoped caching reduced API overhead and improved model latency.
speedrun: The latest advancements in the Codex agent loop highlight how implementing WebSockets and connection-scoped caching can significantly enhance API performance. These changes led to a reduction in API overhead and improved model latency, making interactions smoother and faster. With these optimizations, developers can expect more efficient workflows in their applications. This matters now as the demand for real-time processing continues to grow across various industries.
why_it_matters: Developers will experience faster response times, allowing for more seamless user interactions and improved productivity.
This shift indicates a broader trend towards optimizing real-time data handling, which could influence future API designs and user expectations.

lenses.eli12: The Codex agent loop is like a fast lane for data, allowing information to travel quickly between applications. By using WebSockets, it keeps connections open for instant communication, while connection-scoped caching stores frequently used data nearby. This matters because it means apps can respond faster, making everyday tasks easier for users.
lenses.pm: For product managers, the improvements in the Codex agent loop mean users will face fewer delays when interacting with applications. The enhanced efficiency could lower operational costs while improving user satisfaction. This could encourage teams to prioritize real-time features in their product roadmaps.
lenses.engineer: From a technical perspective, the integration of WebSockets allows for persistent connections that reduce the overhead typically associated with HTTP requests. Coupled with connection-scoped caching, which minimizes redundant data retrieval, these changes can significantly lower latency. This could lead to a more responsive system, but engineers should monitor the impact on resource usage.
